there is a real number $ x $ greater than 1 that is not an integer, and satisfies the inequality:

$$
\{x\}+\left\{x^{3}\right\}+\left\{x^{5}\right\}+\cdots+\left\{x^{99}\right\}<\frac{1}{2^{99}}
$$

where $ \{x\} $ denotes the fractional part of $ x $. this inequality involves the sum of fractional parts of powers of $ x $, starting from $ x $, then $ x^3 $, $ x^5 $, and so on, up to $ x^{99} $. the fractional part of any real number $ y $ is defined as $ \{y\} = y - \lfloor y \rfloor $, where $ \lfloor y \rfloor $ is the greatest integer less than or equal to $ y $. this means that the fractional part of any number is always between 0 (inclusive) and 1 (exclusive). 

the sum of fractional parts is less than $ \frac{1}{2^{99}} $, which is a very small number. this suggests that the fractional parts of the powers of $ x $ must be extremely small. since $ x > 1 $, the powers of $ x $ grow rapidly, and the fractional parts of these powers must be very small to make the sum less than $ \frac{1}{2^{99}} $. 

this is possible if $ x $ is chosen such that the fractional parts of $ x $, $ x^3 $, $ x^5 $, ..., $ x^{99} $ are all very close to zero. for example, if $ x $ is slightly greater than 1, say $ x = 1 + \epsilon $, where $ \epsilon $ is a small positive number, then the fractional part of $ x $ is $ \epsilon $, and the fractional parts of higher powers of $ x $ will depend on the value of $ \epsilon $. 

if $ \epsilon $ is very small, then the fractional parts of $ x^3 $, $ x^5 $, ..., $ x^{99} $ will also be very small. for instance, if $ \epsilon $ is less than $ \frac{1}{2} $, then $ x^3 $ will have a fractional part less than $ \frac{1}{2}
